# Feature Specification: Irrigation Event Calendar

**Feature Branch**: `001-irrigation-event-calendar`

**Created**: 2026-07-04

**Status**: Draft

**Input**: User description: "I want to build a user interface to view the history of events generated by the irrigation system at my house. The system has several different zones and all ON and OFF events for each zone is available via a REST API. The API spec is available in the irrigation-events-server-api.yaml file and the events can be retrieved by making GET calls to the /irrigation-events endpoint. The events should be viewable in a calendar interface that has day and week views. I want the irrigation events to be displayed as timespans similar to how meetings are displayed on an Outlook calendar. It should be easy to switch between views and it should be easy to move forward and backwards through the days and weeks. Use FullCalendar as a reference for what the calendar should look like. The calendar should be view only."

## User Scenarios & Testing *(mandatory)*

### User Story 1 - View Daily Irrigation History (Priority: P1)

As a homeowner, I want to see all irrigation events that occurred on a specific day displayed as time blocks on a calendar, so I can understand exactly when and which zones ran.

**Why this priority**: This is the core use case — without the ability to see events on a single day, the feature delivers no value. Day view provides the most granular, immediately useful information.

**Independent Test**: Load the app, navigate to a day with known irrigation activity, and confirm each zone's run appears as a labeled time block spanning its start and end time. This delivers complete value on its own.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** the calendar is on day view for a date that has irrigation events, **When** the page loads, **Then** each irrigation event is displayed as a vertical time block spanning its start and end time within the day's time grid.
2. **Given** multiple zones ran on the same day, **When** viewing the day, **Then** each zone's event is displayed as a distinct, labeled block (showing the zone name) and overlapping events are visually distinguishable.
3. **Given** an event has no recorded end time (zone is currently running), **When** viewing the day, **Then** the event block extends to the current time and is visually indicated as in-progress.
4. **Given** the day has no irrigation events, **When** viewing the day, **Then** the calendar displays an empty time grid with no event blocks.

---

### User Story 2 - View Weekly Irrigation History (Priority: P2)

As a homeowner, I want to see all irrigation events across a full week at a glance, so I can identify patterns and verify the schedule is running as expected.

**Why this priority**: The week view provides the pattern-recognition and schedule-verification value that complements the detailed day view. It is high priority but depends on the day view infrastructure being in place.

**Independent Test**: Navigate to week view and confirm all days of the week display their irrigation events as time blocks. Clicking or inspecting a block shows the zone name and duration.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** the calendar is in week view, **When** the page loads, **Then** a 7-day grid is displayed with each day's irrigation events shown as time blocks proportional to their duration.
2. **Given** the week view is displayed, **When** the user looks at any day column, **Then** that column shows the same events that would appear if navigating to the day view for that date.
3. **Given** a zone ran for a short duration (e.g., 5 minutes), **When** viewing week view, **Then** the event block is still visible and labeled, even if compact.

---

### User Story 3 - Navigate Forward and Backward in Time (Priority: P1)

As a homeowner, I want to move forward and backward through days and weeks using clear navigation controls, so I can review historical irrigation activity for any date range without friction.

**Why this priority**: Navigation is foundational — without it, the user is stuck on today's date and cannot review history. Elevated to P1 because it is required for the feature to be useful at all.

**Independent Test**: From any view, click the back and forward navigation controls and verify the displayed date range updates correctly and new events load for the new date range.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** the calendar is in day view, **When** the user clicks the "next" control, **Then** the calendar advances to the following day and loads events for that day.
2. **Given** the calendar is in day view, **When** the user clicks the "previous" control, **Then** the calendar moves to the prior day and loads events for that day.
3. **Given** the calendar is in week view, **When** the user clicks "next" or "previous", **Then** the calendar advances or retreats by exactly one full week.
4. **Given** the user is on any view, **When** they click a "Today" control, **Then** the calendar returns to the current day/week.

---

### User Story 4 - Switch Between Day and Week Views (Priority: P2)

As a homeowner, I want to switch between day and week views with a single click, so I can choose the level of detail that best suits my current need.

**Why this priority**: View switching is essential for usability, but the app can deliver initial value with a single fixed view. Kept at P2 because it enhances rather than enables the core experience.

**Independent Test**: Click the "Day" and "Week" toggle controls and verify the calendar layout changes while preserving the current date context.

**Acceptance Scenarios**:

1. **Given** the calendar is in day view, **When** the user clicks the "Week" control, **Then** the calendar switches to week view showing the week containing the currently displayed day.
2. **Given** the calendar is in week view, **When** the user clicks the "Day" control, **Then** the calendar switches to day view showing the first day of the currently displayed week.
3. **Given** the user switches views, **When** they look at the header, **Then** the date range label updates to reflect the new view (e.g., "Mon Jul 4, 2026" for day; "Jul 1–7, 2026" for week).

---

### Edge Cases

- What happens when the API is unavailable or returns an error? The calendar displays an error message in place of events; the time grid remains visible.
- What happens when an event spans midnight (e.g., starts at 11:45 PM and ends at 12:10 AM)? The event is split across the two days or rendered at the boundary — whichever is consistent with the calendar grid.
- What if a zone has overlapping events (data inconsistency)? Both events are rendered; no data is silently dropped.
- What if the date range being viewed contains a very large number of events (e.g., hundreds of zones across a week)? All events are rendered; performance may degrade but the data must not be truncated.
- What if only the zone's OFF time was recorded (the ON event is missing)? The event is displayed anchored to the recorded OFF time with a warning indicator communicating that the start of the run was not captured.
- What if only the zone's ON time was recorded (the OFF event is missing) and the zone is no longer actively running? The event is displayed from the recorded ON time with a warning indicating the end of the run was not captured.
- What if the OFF event is missing and it cannot be determined whether the zone is currently running? The event is displayed from its recorded start time with a distinct warning that its current state is indeterminate.
- What if an event has neither a start nor end timestamp? Such events MUST still appear in the event list with a clear indicator that no timing information is available; they MUST NOT be silently dropped.
- What if the API returns an event whose startTimestamp is before the visible window (e.g., a zone that started at 11:00 AM is returned when the query window starts at noon)? This is expected API behavior — the event is rendered from its actual startTimestamp across the full 24-hour grid. The block appears correctly at 11:00 AM even though noon was the query boundary.

## Clarifications

### Session 2026-07-04

- Q: What portion of the day should the time grid display? → A: Full 24-hour grid (midnight to midnight), vertically scrollable.
- Q: Should zones be color-coded for visual distinction? → A: Yes — each zone is assigned a distinct, consistent color across all views and dates.
- Q: Which view should the app open to by default? → A: Day view showing today's date.
- Context: The API returns events that *overlap* the queried time window, not only events that fall entirely within it. An event that started before the `startTimestamp` query parameter (e.g., a zone that turned on at 11:00 AM returned when querying from noon) will be included in the response so the complete event can be rendered. The UI must render all returned events at their actual timestamps.

## Requirements *(mandatory)*

### Functional Requirements

- **FR-001**: The system MUST display irrigation events as time-block spans on a time-grid calendar. When both start and end times are present, each block's vertical position and height correspond to the event's duration. When only one timestamp is available, the event MUST still be rendered at the known time with a visual indication of the missing bound.
- **FR-002**: The calendar MUST support a day view showing all events for a single date.
- **FR-003**: The calendar MUST support a week view showing all events across a 7-day period (Monday–Sunday or Sunday–Saturday, defaulting to Sunday–Saturday).
- **FR-004**: Each event block MUST display the zone name (derived from the event title).
- **FR-005**: The calendar MUST provide "previous" and "next" navigation controls to move backward and forward by one day (in day view) or one week (in week view).
- **FR-006**: The calendar MUST provide a "Today" control that returns the view to the current date.
- **FR-007**: The calendar MUST provide a view switcher control allowing the user to select between day view and week view.
- **FR-008**: When switching views, the calendar MUST preserve date context (the currently visible date remains visible in the new view).
- **FR-009**: The calendar MUST fetch events from the backend API for the date range currently visible, including at least the full start-of-day to end-of-day range for all visible days. The API may return events whose timestamps extend outside the queried range (events that overlap the window boundary); such events MUST be rendered at their actual start and end times — not clipped to the visible window.
- **FR-010**: Events where `currentlyOn` is true (zone is actively running) MUST be visually distinguished from completed events.
- **FR-011**: Events with incomplete timing data MUST be visually distinguished from normal events. Three warning scenarios MUST each be communicated to the user: (1) only the end of the zone's run is known — the start was not recorded; (2) only the start of the zone's run is known — the end was not recorded; (3) the zone's run start is known but its end and current state are indeterminate.
- **FR-012**: The calendar MUST be read-only — no user interaction shall create, modify, or delete events.
- **FR-013**: While event data is loading, the calendar MUST display a loading indicator so the user knows data is being fetched.
- **FR-014**: If the API request fails, the calendar MUST display a user-friendly error message.
- **FR-015**: Multiple zones running simultaneously MUST be rendered as side-by-side blocks within the same time column (or otherwise visually distinguishable) — events MUST NOT obscure one another.
- **FR-016**: The time grid MUST span the full 24-hour day (midnight to midnight) and MUST be vertically scrollable so any hour of the day is reachable without the grid being cropped.
- **FR-017**: Each zone MUST be assigned a distinct color that is applied consistently to all of that zone's event blocks across all views and all dates — the same zone always appears in the same color.

### Key Entities

- **Irrigation Event**: A single on/off cycle for one irrigation zone. Attributes: zone name (title, always present), start time (may be absent if the ON event was not recorded), end time (may be absent if the zone is still running or the OFF event was not recorded), device/zone identifier (always present), active status (optional — indicates the zone is currently running), warning type (optional, one of three states: ON event missing, OFF event missing, or OFF event missing with indeterminate current state).
- **Zone**: A distinct irrigation area identified by its device ID and displayed by its title name. Each zone is assigned a unique display color that remains consistent across all views and dates. Multiple zones may run simultaneously.
- **Calendar View**: A time-grid display mode — either day (single date) or week (7 consecutive dates) — with a navigable date range.

## Success Criteria *(mandatory)*

### Measurable Outcomes

- **SC-001**: A user can navigate from today to any date within the past 30 days in 5 or fewer interactions (clicks or taps).
- **SC-002**: All irrigation events within the visible date range appear on the calendar within 3 seconds of the view loading on a typical home network connection.
- **SC-003**: The calendar is usable on a mobile phone screen (portrait orientation) without horizontal scrolling of the page chrome — the time grid may scroll vertically.
- **SC-004**: Switching between day and week views takes no more than 1 interaction (a single click/tap on the view control).
- **SC-005**: When two or more zones ran simultaneously, both are visible on the calendar without one completely hiding the other.
- **SC-006**: A user unfamiliar with the system can identify which zone ran, when it started, and how long it ran, for any event, without any additional instructions.

## Assumptions

- The homeowner is the sole user of this interface; multi-user access control and authentication are out of scope.
- The backend API is accessible from the same network as the user's browser; no authentication headers are required for API calls.
- Event data is complete and authoritative — the UI is not responsible for reconciling or correcting event data.
- Zones are identified by their `title` field from the API; no separate zone-name lookup is required.
- The week view defaults to Sunday–Saturday week boundaries, consistent with common North American calendar conventions.
- The app opens in day view showing today's date by default.
- When an event is flagged as currently active, the UI treats the current time as the provisional end for rendering purposes so the in-progress block extends to "now".
- A zone's active status may not be present on every event; when absent and no end time is recorded, the calendar treats the event's current state as uncertain and renders it with the appropriate warning.
- All event timestamps, when present, follow the standard date-time format (including timezone offset) as defined by the ISO 8601 standard.
- No pagination of events is required — the API returns all overlapping events in a single response. An event that started before the `startTimestamp` query parameter (or ends after `endTimestamp`) may be included if it overlaps the requested range; the UI renders all returned events at their actual timestamps.
- Historical data is available for at least the past 90 days via the API.
